title: ACP search delegates unescaped user input into a shell command (RCE)
description:
## Severity
Critical
## Dimension
security
## Location
src/acp.rs:1685-1720 (acp_search)
## Violation
`acp_search` constructs shell commands by direct string interpolation of model/tool-call-supplied arguments, then ships them through `acp_bash` → `terminal/create` for execution. There is no quoting, escaping, or allowlisting:

```rust
format!("find {path} -name '{pattern}' -type f 2>/dev/null | head -100")
...
let _ = write!(cmd, " --type {ft}");
let _ = write!(cmd, " --glob '{g}'");
let _ = write!(cmd, " '{pattern}' {path} 2>/dev/null | head -200");
```

Any value containing `;`, `$(...)`, backticks, or a closing `'` breaks out of the intended command. A glob like `' ; rm -rf ~ ; '` injects an arbitrary command into the user's terminal subprocess. Tool arguments come from the model — and from upstream prompt content — so a malicious upstream provider response or prompt-injected file can pivot to RCE on the user's box.
## Mandated refactor
Stop building a shell string entirely. Pass `find` / `rg` / `ls` argv as a typed argv vector to the ACP `terminal/create` request (or to a `Command::new("rg").args([...])` path). If a shell command must be constructed, allowlist `pattern`/`path`/`type`/`glob` with a strict regex (`^[A-Za-z0-9_./*?\[\]-]+$` for pattern, no shell metacharacters in `type`), reject the call on mismatch, and route through `shell-escape` / `shlex::quote` for any remaining substitution. The current head-100 pipeline assumes shell semantics — split it into argv plus a Rust-side truncation of the captured output.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
